title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR introduces coupon discount support but contains a critical correctness bug that causes `discount_amount` to always return 0, a financial safety bug that allows negative charge amounts, a PCI-relevant debug log that emits the full card payload in plaintext, and silent error-swallowing that hides coupon failures from the caller. Naming, style, and test coverage also need attention before merge.

findings.title: DEBUG log emits full charge body including card data to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serialises the entire request body – which includes the card number, CVV, and expiry – into application logs. This is a PCI-DSS violation and must be removed before merge. No card or customer PII should ever be written to a log at any level.

findings.title: `discount_amount` is always 0 – original amount overwritten before subtraction
findings.message: Line 24 sets `payload.amount = finalAmount`, so by the time line 36 computes `payload.amount - finalAmount` both operands are identical and the result is always 0. The fix is to capture the original amount before it is mutated: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` and then compute `originalAmount - finalAmount` in the response.

findings.title: Swallowed coupon exception silently charges full price with no client feedback
findings.message: The inner `try/catch` discards every error from `doStuff` (including programmer mistakes) with `// TODO`. The charge then proceeds at the original `finalAmount` but `discount_amount` (already broken) would still be reported as 0. The caller gets no indication that coupon application failed. At minimum this should propagate an appropriate error response; swallowing exceptions on a payment path is a data-integrity and user-trust risk.

findings.title: `FixedCouponStrategy` can produce a negative charge amount
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt` has no floor guard. If a TENOFF ($10) coupon is applied to a $3 order the result is -700 (in minor currency units). Sending a negative amount to a payment gateway will either fail unpredictably or, worse, trigger a refund. Add `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)` and decide whether a zero-amount charge should be allowed or rejected earlier.

findings.title: `response: any` discards type safety on the returned transaction
findings.message: Casting `transaction` to `any` in order to bolt on two extra fields defeats TypeScript's guarantees and will suppress any future type errors on the response shape. Define a proper response type (e.g. `type ChargeResponse = typeof transaction & { discount_amount: number; coupon_code?: string }`) or use object spread: `res.status(201).json({ ...transaction, discount_amount: ..., coupon_code: ... })`.

findings.title: Public export named `doStuff` – entirely non-descriptive
findings.message: This is the primary public API of the module and is called from the controller, yet its name gives no indication of what it does. Rename to something like `applyCoupon(amount: number, couponCode?: string): number`. The same problem extends to internal identifiers: `p`, `amt`, `tmp`, `result2`, `x`.

findings.title: Coupon codes hard-coded in source; visible to anyone with repository access
findings.message: All coupon codes and their discount values are embedded in source code. Anyone with read access to the repository can discover valid codes. These should be stored in a database or secrets-aware configuration, looked up at runtime, and ideally hashed or otherwise protected. Hard-coding also means adding or expiring a coupon requires a code deploy.

findings.title: Long if-else chain uses `==` (loose equality) instead of `===`
findings.message: TypeScript/JavaScript best-practice and most linting rules require strict equality (`===`). Use `===` throughout. The if-else chain itself should also be replaced with a lookup table (a plain `Record<string, AbstractCouponStrategy>` or `Map`) to make adding/removing coupons a one-liner and remove the repeated structural pattern.

findings.title: `var` declarations used instead of `const`
findings.message: `var x = ...` (line 16) and `var tmp = ...` (line 61) should be `const`. `var` has function scope and hoisting behaviour that is a common source of bugs; the project is already using `let`/`const` everywhere else.

findings.title: Unknown coupon code logged at DEBUG – enables coupon enumeration via log scraping
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` logs the raw user-supplied string. This (a) leaks attempted coupon codes to logs, (b) is a leftover debug statement that should be removed or replaced with a structured, levelled logger, and (c) does not rate-limit guessing – combined with the log output an attacker can enumerate valid codes by observing which attempts produce no log entry.

findings.title: Abstract class with a single method is over-engineered for the current use case
findings.message: `AbstractCouponStrategy` adds a class hierarchy solely to hold `apply(amount): number`. A TypeScript interface or a plain function type (`type DiscountFn = (amount: number) => number`) achieves the same polymorphism with less boilerplate. The factory and strategies can be simplified to a map of functions, removing ~30 lines of class ceremony.

findings.title: Commented-out dead code should be removed
findings.message: The `FREESHIP` block (lines 48-51) is commented out and the inline comment says it is not used. Dead code in a payment-critical module creates confusion about intent. Remove it; version control preserves the history if it is ever needed.

findings.title: Unnecessary `theData` intermediate variable; `couponCode` bypasses schema validation
findings.message: Renaming `req.body` to `theData` adds noise without value. More importantly, `couponCode` is extracted from the raw body _before_ Zod validation runs, so it is never validated or sanitised by the schema. Move `couponCode` extraction to after `chargeSchema.parse`, or add it as an optional field in `chargeSchema` so its type and format are enforced alongside the rest of the payload.

findings.title: No unit tests for coupon math – acknowledged as TODO in the PR
findings.message: The PR description explicitly marks the test checkbox unchecked. Given that the discount calculation has a correctness bug that would have been caught by even a trivial test (`expect(applyCoupon(1000, 'WELCOME10')).toBe(900)`), this should be a hard blocker for merge, not a follow-up. Tests for negative-amount edge cases and unknown coupon handling are equally essential.
